label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The passage is about how they look for missing children. The first thing they do is put an APB, which goes to all police stations. Then they call hospitals, maybe the child was in an accident. Then they try parents, friends, relatives, follow movements, check local and national papers. Other things like posters, TV. In America, there's a magazine with photos of missing kids. But then it says, "Of course, with nearly two million missing children every year, we can't do all these things for everyone. We haven't got the time, or the money, or the people who work for it." The question is asking, according to the passage, when looking for a missing child, they... The options are A to D. Let me go through each option. Option A: can always find him. The passage doesn't say they can always find them. It actually mentions that there are two million missing every year, implying that they can't find all of them. So A is probably wrong. Option B: couldn't always try every way mentioned above. The passage says "we can't do all these things for everyone. We haven't got the time, or the money, or the people who work for it." So they mention that they can't do all the methods for every case. So B seems correct. Option C: just try one way or another. The passage lists multiple steps they take (APB, hospitals, parents, friends, papers, posters, TV, magazine). So they don't just try one way. So C is probably wrong. Option D: won't give up until they find him. The passage doesn't say they never give up. It just says they use various methods and sometimes can't do all because of resources. It doesn't mention persistence until found.
